coredata.dc:title: Nano-island-like heterojunction of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots anchoring on titanium oxide nano sheets@Cu2O for enhancing visible light-driving photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Lingyun
Liang, Zhanming
Yu, Yuanyuan
Hou, Hewei
Liu, Xi
Min, Douyong
coredata.dc:description: Cu2O and TiO2 are promising for photocatalysis owing to their lower cost and excellent performance. Unfortunately, the efficiency of light utilization by Cu2O and TiO2 is still low. Herein, the nano-island-like heterojunction of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots anchoring on titanium oxide nano sheets@Cu2O (NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O) was synthesized through the hydrothermal and chemical precipitation process and its photocatalytic performance was systematically characterized. Benefiting from the unique heterojunction, NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O exhibited a superior photocatalytic performance. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy showed that NCQDs/TNSs nanoclusters were decorated on Cu2O nanoparticle surface and formed heterojunctions. The UV–Vis-DRS spectra indicated that NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O exhibited the strong adsorption peaks between 400 and 650 nm. Under simulated sunlight irradiation, NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O exhibited excellent photocatalytic degradation performance on methyl orange. For example, the degradation was completed within 20 min and the photo-degradation capacity of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O remained 90.1 % after four recycling cycles. Furthermore, the potential mechanism for the photo-induced degradation was proposed based on the active substances identified through the electron spin resonance. Conclusively, this study provides a novel perspective on the role of NCQDs and prepares the way for the design of the biomass-based photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Heterojunction
Photo-catalyst
Wastewater treatment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported financially by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2022YFC2105503 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32371814 , 31400514 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province ( 2018JJA130224 ), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp and Papermaking and Pollution Control ( ZR201805-7 ), and the Opening Project of National Enterprise Technology Center of Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd , Nanning 530007, China.

The work was supported financially by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFC2105503), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32371814, 31400514), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2018JJA130224), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp and Papermaking and Pollution Control (ZR201805-7), and the Opening Project of National Enterprise Technology Center of Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co. Ltd, Nanning 530007, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic nano island like structure of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O; (b) SEM of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs (inset); (c–f) SEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping of NCQDs/TNSs; (g–j) SEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O.
(a) XRD patterns of TNSs, NCQDs/TNSs, Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O; (b) XPS spectrum of Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O and the high-resolution spectra (c) Cu2p (c), (d) O1s, (e) Ti2p.
(a) TEM of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O; (b) HR-TEM image of Cu2O and SAED (inset); (c) HR-TEM image of NCQDs/TNSs.
(a) The absorption of samples; (b) the corresponding plots of transformed Kubelka-Munk function vs photo energy of samples.

Time-dependent UV–vis absorption spectra of 20 mg/L MO degraded by TNSs (a), and NCQDs/TNSs (b); (c) the corresponding degradation curves and kinetic curves of TNSs and NCQD/TNSs for photocatalytic degradation of MO, respectively. The UV absorption changes of 20 mg/L MO degraded by Cu2O (d), and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O (e); (f) the corresponding degradation curves and kinetic curves of Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O for photocatalytic degradation of MO.

(a) Photocatalytic removal of MO (20 mg/L) through Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O in the presence of different scavengers under the irradiation. ESR spectra of Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O under dark and irradiation: (b) TEMP-O2 −, (c) TEMPO-h+.
The potential degradation mechanism of MO catalyzed by the NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O under the simulated irradiation.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O.

head.ce:title: Nano-island-like heterojunction of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots anchoring on titanium oxide nano sheets@Cu2O for enhancing visible light-driving photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp & Papermaking and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd, Nanning 530007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd, Nanning 530007, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cu2O and TiO2 are promising for photocatalysis owing to their lower cost and excellent performance. Unfortunately, the efficiency of light utilization by Cu2O and TiO2 is still low. Herein, the nano-island-like heterojunction of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots anchoring on titanium oxide nano sheets@Cu2O (NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O) was synthesized through the hydrothermal and chemical precipitation process and its photocatalytic performance was systematically characterized. Benefiting from the unique heterojunction, NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O exhibited a superior photocatalytic performance. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy showed that NCQDs/TNSs nanoclusters were decorated on Cu2O nanoparticle surface and formed heterojunctions. The UV–Vis-DRS spectra indicated that NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O exhibited the strong adsorption peaks between 400 and 650 nm. Under simulated sunlight irradiation, NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O exhibited excellent photocatalytic degradation performance on methyl orange. For example, the degradation was completed within 20 min and the photo-degradation capacity of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O remained 90.1 % after four recycling cycles. Furthermore, the potential mechanism for the photo-induced degradation was proposed based on the active substances identified through the electron spin resonance. Conclusively, this study provides a novel perspective on the role of NCQDs and prepares the way for the design of the biomass-based photocatalyst.

Scheme 1. Photo-degradation of MO catalyzed by NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O and its potential mechanism.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Methyl orange (MO) is a commonly applied dye in the fields of textiles and coatings. It has high chroma [1], complex composition, carcinogenicity and biological toxicity, and constitutes a grave hazard towards public health and the ecological environment [2]. In order to reduce the concentration of dye pollutants in water, traditional treatment techniques including filtering [3], adsorption [4] and oxidation [5], etc. have been widely used. Although they can remove dyes from wastewater, these technologies still have some disadvantages, such as filtration and adsorption methods cannot really degrade the pollutants but only enrich them, and chemical oxidation methods need to use oxidizing agents which are prone to lead to secondary pollution [6].

Over the past few decades, the photo-catalysis has gradually become a favorable strategy to solve water pollution problems owing to its lower cost, great sustainability, and superior removal rate [7]. Semiconductor-based photo-catalysts have attracted numerous attention of research for solar power conversion and environmental applications. Among various photo-catalysts, copper oxide (Cu2O), as a ubiquitous photo-catalyst, is an inexpensive, non-toxic p-type semiconductor [8]. It has a unique powerful absorption coefficient and electrical conductivity qualities, allowing it to degrade organic substances when exposed to visible light, which make it a potential substitute for other photocatalysts [9,10], such as TiO2.

Unfortunately, the photocatalytic performance of Cu2O is severely limited under light by rapid recombination and instability of light-induced electron-hole pairs [11,12]. One strategy is to combine Cu2O with an appropriate catalyst, such as Fe2O3 [13], Mn(OH)2 [14] and carbon dots [15], etc. However, these catalysts have certain shortcomings such as easy agglomeration, complicated recycling operations, and unstable structures in practical applications, which result in poor photocatalytic performance. It has been reported that Cu2O has higher valence and conduction bands than TiO2, which is thermodynamically favorable for exciting the electrons and holes transfers, resulting in enhanced carriers separation and reduced their recombination, and it can also further decrease the photo-corrosion of Cu2O and improve its stability [13,16]. TiO2 is a low-cost, structurally stable n-type catalyst, which can be combined with Cu2O to construct a heterojunction as an efficient composite photo-catalyst [17]. Nevertheless, TiO2 has a wide band gap and low light utilization efficiency. To overcome these shortcomings, TiO2 has been reported to be an effective photo-catalyst with broad spectral response after it is composited with carbon quantum dots [18].

In this investigation, the nano-island-like NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O heterojunction photo-catalyst was prepared through the hydrothermal and chemical precipitation process. NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O exhibited an excellent performance in simulating solar light absorption, efficient photo-induced carrier separation, and stability. The catalyst exhibited excellent photocatalytic degradation performance for MO, and the degradation efficiency remained over 90 % after four recycling cycles. Furthermore, the potential photocatalytic mechanism depending on the active radicals generated by NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O during the photocatalytic process was presented. In conclusion, this study offers a viable technique for optimizing the performance of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O heterojunction as a photo-catalyst.

Kraft lignin was obtained from Guitang Company (Guangxi, China). Ethylenediamine anhydrous (EDA), hydrofluoric acid (HF), potassium iodide (KI), isopropanol (IPA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), ethanol and methyl orange (MO) were obtained from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Cupric chloride (CuCl2) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were analytic grade and were used without further purification.

NCQDs were prepared according to the reported procedure [19]. Briefly, 0.5 g of Kraft lignin (KL) was sonicated 50 mL 20 % of EDA for 10 min. Subsequently, the suspension was added to a PTFE-lined stainless steel reactor and autoclaved at 180 °C for 12 h. Upon the completion of reaction, the suspension was cooled to ambient temperature and passed through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to eliminate aggregated pellets. Subsequently, dialysis was performed on the filtrate for a week using a dialysis bag (MWCO 3500 Da). NCQDs were obtained after two days of freeze-drying.

TiO2 nanosheets (TNSs) were produced using the published approach [20]. Briefly, 25 mL of titanium butoxide (TBT) was mixed with 9 mL of deionized water, and then 4.5 mL of HF was added into the suspension. The suspension was stirred at ambient temperature for 0.5 h and then transferred into a PTFE-lined stainless steel reactor by autoclaving at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling, the anatase TiO2 nanocrystals were obtained as white precipitates which were washed by ethanol and deionized water to remove impurities. The precipitates were calcinated at 550 °C for 2 h to obtain TNSs.

Cu2O was prepared according to the reported method [21]. 67 mg of CuCl2 and 0.5 g of sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were dissolved in 65 mL of deionized water. Then, 10 mL of 0.2 M NaOH was introduced. After that, 2.5 mg of 1 M NH2OH·HCl was added to the precursors. The reaction was completed at ambient temperature for 1.5 h. The suspension was centrifuged to collect the product which was washed several times with a mixture of water and ethanol. Cu2O was obtained after 6 h of vacuum drying at 60 °C.

NCQDs/TNSs were prepared as follows. 95 mg TNSs was suspended in 100 mL ethanol and then sonicated for 10 min. 5 mg NCQDs was suspended in 5 mL deionized water and then sonicated for 30 min. Subsequently, the NCQDs suspension was mixed with the TNS suspension and stirred for 2 h. The mixture was dried in an oven at 105 °C and then calcined at 550 °C for 2 h to obtain NCQDs/TNSs.

NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O was prepared as follows. 7.15 mg of NCQDs/TNSs was suspended in 20 mL deionized water and then sonicated for 10 min (solution A). Subsequently, 67 mg of CuCl2 was dissolved in 45 mL deionized water (solution B). After that, the solution A was mixed with the solution B to obtain the solution C. 0.5 g of SDS were dissolved in the solution C. 10 mL of 0.2 M NaOH was added into the solution C. Then, 2.5 mg of 1 M NH2OH·HCl was added to the precursor. The reaction was completed at ambient temperature for 1.5 h. The suspension was centrifuged to collect the product which was washed with a mixture of water and ethanol several times. NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O was obtained after vacuum drying at 60 °C for 6 h (Scheme 1).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to observe the morphology and particle size of samples. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation was used to determine the crystallinity of samples. The element compositions and surface chemical state were examined using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI+, Thermo Fisher Scientific, America) with a monochromatic Al Kα source. The UV spectra of samples were recorded by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Specond plus 50, Analytik Jena, Germany).

The photocatalytic performance of samples on MO degradation was evaluated under the simulated light irradiation. In each experiment, 10 mg of catalyst (TNSs, NCQDs/TNSs, Cu2O, and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O) was applied in 50 mL of MO solution (20 ppm). Adsorption equilibrium was achieved in dark for 1 h. Subsequently, the photocatalytic degradation was occurred under the simulated sunlight irradiation at the room temperature. Every 10 min, 2.5 mL of solution was collected and filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Each experiment was triplicated and the mean and standard deviation were presented. The degradation rate of MO was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
Degradation rate
%
=
C t
/
C 0
× 100 % =
A t
/
A 0
× 100 %
where,
C 0
is the initial MO concentration at t = 0;
C t
is the MO concentration during reaction at the time of t;
A 0
and
A t
are the corresponding absorbance.

Because the initial concentration of pollutants was extremely low, the reactions followed the Langmuir-Hinshelwood model with first-order kinetics [22]. The kinetic was calculated using Eq. (2).
(2)
− ln
C t
/
C 0
= − ln
A t
/
A 0
= kt
where,
C 0
is the initial concentration of pollutants;
C t
is the concentration at t. k is the degradation rate constant (min−1);
A 0
and
A t
are the corresponding UV absorbance.

Scavengers i.e. KI, IPA, and BQ were employed to determine the activity of hole (h+), hydroxyl radical (OH), and superoxide radical (O2 −), respectively. 10 mg of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O catalyst was applied to 50 mL of MO solution (20 ppm). Adsorption equilibrium was achieved in dark for 1 h. And then the scavengers (KI, IPA, and BQ) were added to the reaction system, where the concentration of the scavengers were controlled at 1 mmol/L. Subsequently, the photocatalytic degradation was occurred as the simulated sunlight was irradiated at the room temperature. Every 10 min, 2.5 mL of solution was withdrawn and filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. An electron spin resonance spectrometer (ESR) was applied to reveal the existence of O2 and h+ radicals during photocatalytic degradation.

The stability of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O was detected by the recycling test. When the photodegradation was completed, the NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O was collected by centrifugation and washed 5 times with deionized water and then 5 times with ethanol. Subsequently, the recovered photocatalyst was used for the next cycle of the photocatalytic degradation.

Fig. 1a shows the schematic structure of the as-prepared NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O catalyst. Fig. 1b shows that NCQDs were anchored on TNSs which exhibited a size ranging from 20 to 50 nm and the synthesized NCQDs/TNSs was decorated on the surface of octadecahedral Cu2O particles. NCQDs/TNSs was aggregated and dispersed on the surface of Cu2O to form NCQDs/TNSs nano islands with prominent structures. Fig. 1c–f shows the distribution of carbon, titanium and nitrogen, confirming the formation of NCQDs/TNSs. Fig. 1g–j shows the distribution of copper, titanium and carbon, confirming the generation of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O. The results indicated that the promising NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O heterojunction with well-designed structure was successfully fabricated.

Fig. 2a shows the XRD analysis of TNSs, Cu2O, NCQDs/TNSs, and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O. The characteristic diffraction peaks assigned to anatase TiO2 were identified from TNSs and NCQDs/TNSs [23]. The diffraction peaks corresponding to CuO and Cu were identified from Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O. In addition, the characteristic diffraction peaks attributed to the face-centered Cu2O phase were identified from Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O [24]. Interestingly, a weak diffraction peaks responding to anatase TiO2 were observed from NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O, confirming the successful decoration of NCQDs/TNSs on Cu2O.

Fig. 2b shows the XPS analysis of Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O. The presence of Ti, O and Cu elements in NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O was confirmed. The presence of C in Cu2O was explained by the environmental carbon [25]. Fig. 2c–e illustrates the high-resolution spectra corresponding to Cu2p peak, O1s peak and Ti2p peak of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2c, two intensive peaks at 932.4 eV and 952.3 eV were corresponded to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 in cuprous oxide, respectively, while the peak at 946.6 eV was attributed to a satellite peak from Cu2+ [26]. Fig. 2d shows that three high intensive peaks at 530.4, 531.6, and 533.2 eV were assigned to TiO, CO and OH of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O, respectively. Fig. 2e demonstrates that the intensive peaks at 458.4, and 464.1 eV were attributed to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O, respectively. The results revealed that a minor fraction of surface Cu2O on the nanoparticles can be oxidized to CuO during the preparation process. The small amounts of CuO present on Cu2O surface has been reported to enhance its stability [27,28].

Fig. 3a shows the TEM image of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O, which demonstrates the nanoclusters (NCQDs/TNSs) decorating on the surface of Cu2O nano particle. Fig. 3b–c shows the HRTEM images and lattice diffraction patterns of Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs (marked region). Fig. 3b shows the typical HRTEM images of the Cu2O interfacial region of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O. The appearance of electron diffraction (SAED) patterns and (100), (110), (111), (200), and (220) planes of Cu2O confirm the polycrystalline structure of Cu2O. The HRTEM image of the NCQDs/TNSs nanocluster (Fig. 3c) verified its highly crystalline structure. It can be observed two sets of crystal lattice planes with d-spacing of ~0.235 and ~0.21 nm, corresponding to the (001) plane of the anatase TiO2 phase and the lattice of NCQDs [29,30]. Fig. 3a shows that the Cu2O phase gradually transforms into the anatase NCQDs/TNSs phase. This observation suggested that the NCQDs/TNSs nanoclusters were decorated on Cu2O nanoparticle surface and formed heterojunctions, which can potentially facilitate the photoexcitation and electron transfer.

Fig. 4a displays the UV–Vis-DRS spectra of samples. Compared to TNSs and NCQDs/TNSs, the strong adsorption peaks between 400 and 650 nm were observed from Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O, indicating their excellent broad optical responsiveness. In addition, the band gap (Eg) can be calculated by the following Kubelka-Munk equation [15,31].
(3)
α hv
1 n
= A
hv − Eg
where, α is the absorption coefficient; h is Planck constant; v is the incident photon frequency; A is a constant; E is the band gap value; and n represents ½ for a direct transition.

As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the Eg values of TNSs, NCQDs/TNSs, Cu2O, and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O were calculated as 3.23 eV, 3.22 eV, 2.60 eV, and 2.56 eV, respectively. The presence of NCQDs/TNSs producing plasmon resonance effects can effectively decrease the band gap value of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O. The small band gap indicated the easy light trapping and carrier separation, which improved the photocatalytic efficiency of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O [22,33].

Fig. 5a shows that the UV absorbance of the MO solution decreased a little with the reaction time when TNSs were used as the photocatalyst. However, Fig. 5b shows the UV absorption decreased with the reaction time as NCQDs/TNSs were used as the photocatalyst. The results revealed that NCQDs/TNSs possessed better photocatalytic performance than the pristine TNSs which was attributed to the expansion of photo-response induced by NCQDs [34]. Fig. 5c shows that a substantial amount of MO was adsorbed by the photocatalyst. NCQDs/TNSs adsorbed more MO than TNSs which was attributed to the fact that the decoration of NCQDs increased the active adsorption sites [35]. It was calculated that only 14 % of MO was photo-degraded by TNSs in 60 min under the irradiation, however, 21 % of MO was degraded by NCQDs/TNSs. Meanwhile, the degradation rate of MO by NCQDs/TNSs was 0.0032 min−1, which was 1.68 times higher than that of TNSs (0.0019 min−1). The results suggested that the decoration of NCQDs can effectively enhance the photocatalytic performance of TNSs.

The photo-degradation of MO catalyzed by Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O was shown in Fig. 5d and e, respectively. Generally, the catalytic ability of Cu2O was remarkably better than that of TNSs and NCQDs/TNSs. However, the performance of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O was much better than that of Cu2O, which can be explained by the anchor of NCQDs/TNSs on Cu2O photo-induced effectively restrained the recombination quenching of photo-induced electron-hole pairs [36]. Fig. 5f shows that the physical adsorption of MO was slightly increased as NCQDs/TNSs was anchored on Cu2O [37]. The results displayed that 90 % of MO was photo-degraded by Cu2O, however 99 % of MO was degraded by NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O in 60 min under the irradiation. The k value of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O was calculated as 0.1062 min−1 which was 54.39, 32.46 and 2.55 times higher than the counterparts of TNSs (0.0019 min−1), NCQDs/TNSs (0.0032 min−1) and Cu2O (0.0390 min−1), respectively. The results revealed that the heterojunction formed as NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O can effectively promote the photocatalytic performance.

The active species generated in the Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O photocatalytic systems were studied by the radical quenching experiments, respectively. Fig. 6a shows that the different inhibition of photocatalytic degradation of MO was caused by each free radical scavenger. When IPA was applied as the OH scavenger in the photocatalytic degradation process, the MO degradation efficiencies by Cu2O and NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O remained around 70 % and 58 %, respectively, indicating that OH was not the major active substance in the photocatalytic degradation reaction. However, when BQ was applied as the O2 − scavenger, the MO degradation efficiencies was reduced to 21 % and 2 %, respectively, suggesting that O2 − was the main active substance. Furthermore, when KI was applied as the h+ scavenger, the degrading efficiency of MO was reduced to 43 % and 38 %, respectively, indicating that h+ was the major active substance. ESR was utilized to determine the major reactive radicals (Fig. 6b–c). In dark, the signal of TEMP-O2 − was weak. However, within 10 min irradiation, a significant increase in the signal of TEMP-O2 − was detected. In addition, the signal of TEMPO-h+ was consistently stronger under both dark and irradiation. Therefore, the major active substances for photocatalytic degradation were detected as the photo-induced O2 − and h+.

The semiconductor energy band edges have a crucial role in specifying the charge-transfer behavior at interfaces. NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O valence band (VB) edge and conduction band (CB) edge were calculated by the following equation [38].
(4)
E VB
= χ −
E e
+ 0.5
E g
(5)
E CB
=
E VB
−
E g
where, χ is the semiconductor's absolute electronegativity; Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (~4.5 eV); and Eg is the semiconductor bandgap energy [39,40].

The absolute electro-negativities of TNSs and Cu2O were 5.81 and 5.32 eV, respectively. Therefore, the CB and VB edge potentials of Cu2O were estimated to be −0.48 eV and 2.12 eV, respectively. TNSs' CB and VB edge potentials were estimated to be −0.3 eV and 2.92 eV, respectively. Fig. 7 depicts the energy band structure diagram, indicating a heterojunction was formed as TNSs were anchored on Cu2O surface, the photo-induced electrons moved to NCQDs on TNSs, and the rate of photo-generated electron-hole complexation was significantly reduced. Moreover, the tiny band gap encouraged sunlight absorption, thereby accelerating the creation for photogenerated holes and electrons [16,41].

According to the aforementioned findings, a potential mechanism for the photo degradation of MO catalyzed by NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O was proposed in Fig. 7. Briefly, a large number of p-n heterojunctions were formed as NCQDs/TNSs were decorated on the Cu2O surface. Under the simulated irradiation, a substantial amount of electron-hole pairs were generated, the electrons excited on Cu2O were transported from the CB of Cu2O to CB of NCQDs/TNSs, then transferred to NCQDs, while the holes excited on NCQDs/TNSs were transported from the VB of NCQDs/TNSs to the VB of Cu2O [42]. As a result, the heterojunction's electrostatic field and charged potential difference lead to carrier transfer [43,44]. The formation of heterojunction as NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O facilitated the fast transfer of photo-excited carriers, improving the separation rate of electron-hole coupling.

Cu2O was decorated by NCQD/TNS to form a nano-island-like heterojunction configuration, and thus can be effectively applied to aqueous solution. The electrons separated by the CB in NCQDs/TNSs were easily captured by the adsorbed O2 to form superoxide radicals (O2 −), the holes in the VB of Cu2O can react with H2O or OH− species to generate highly active free radicals (OH) [45]. These active oxidative species reacted with organic pollutants to complete the degradation. In addition, Cu2O was protected from photo-corrosion by the instantaneous scavenging of holes [46].

The recycle of NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O was also investigated (Fig. 8 ). After 4 cycles, the photo-degradation rate of MO catalyzed by NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O can remain above 90 %, exhibiting its reasonable stability. It has been reported that nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) used as the photocatalyst can maintain its good performance after 15 times cycles, however, the recycling was accomplished by a column chromatography separation method [47]. In this work, NCQDs anchored on TiO2 nanosheets was decorated on Cu2O to form a powder photocatalyst, which was recovered by the centrifugation separation. Therefore, as a powder catalyst, a portion of catalyst was inevitably lost during the recycling process. Furthermore, Cu2O can be oxidized into CuO during the recycling process, which alleviated the performance of the catalyst. However, Cu2O, as the carrier of heterojunction, had significant visible light absorption, which was promoted by the excellent up-conversion luminescence properties of NCQDs. Therefore, NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O possessed a high light utilization efficiency, excellent catalytic performance and reasonable stability.

Through the process of hydrothermal and chemical precipitation, the nano-island-like NCQD/TNSs@Cu2O heterojunction was synthesized which facilitated the photo-excited electron transfer. The calculation revealed that NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O possessed the lowest bandgap energy and the least recombination quenching of electron-hole pairs, which were directly ascribed to degradation capability. The degradation results illustrated that NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O had excellent photocatalytic degradation rate of 99 % for MO in 20 min. The rate constant of photocatalytic degradation was calculated as 0.1062 min−1. In addition, the catalyst still maintained its good catalytic activity after four times recycling. Due to the facile and eco-friendly synthesis approach and excellent photocatalytic performance, NCQDs/TNSs@Cu2O can be used as an ideal photocatalytic heterojunction for wastewater treatment.

Lingyun Hao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhanming Liang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yuanyuan Yu: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Hewei Hou: Methodology, Investigation. Xi Liu: Methodology, Conceptualization. Douyong Min: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported financially by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFC2105503), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32371814, 31400514), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2018JJA130224), the Foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Clean Pulp and Papermaking and Pollution Control (ZR201805-7), and the Opening Project of National Enterprise Technology Center of Guangxi Bossco Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd, Nanning 530007, China.
